title: Whether ageing performers should be encouraged to stop
summary: Argue whether singers, dancers, and athletes whose powers are visibly declining should be quietly nudged into retirement, or whether they have every right to keep performing.
topic: Whether older performers in visible decline should be encouraged to retire
prompt: Write a 350–500-word argumentative essay about whether public performers — a celebrated singer in Cairo, a leading dancer in Buenos Aires, a long-standing tenor in Naples — should be gently encouraged to retire once their powers visibly fade, or whether they have the right to keep performing for as long as they wish. Use a 'what' cleft, a concession ('Granted, …' or 'Admittedly, …'), at least one passive construction, and one participle clause. Anchor the argument in at least one specific performer, art form, or city. Take a clear position.
hintStarters: What an ageing performer's last decade on stage offers an audience that no younger performer can replicate is…
Granted, the line between dignified late-career performance and the slow public unravelling of a once-great voice is not…
Watching a celebrated Cairo singer at her final scheduled concert last spring, one began to understand that the people in the audience were not there to hear the…

hintVocab.meaningVi: nghệ sĩ biểu diễn lớn tuổi
nghỉ hưu một cách duyên dáng

hintVocab.phrase: to overstay one's welcome on stage
hintVocab.meaningVi: ở lại sân khấu lâu hơn mức được chào đón
buổi biểu diễn cuối / tác phẩm cuối
lòng trung thành của khán giả
sự diễn giải / cách thể hiện
vượt qua đau đớn để tiếp tục

hintVocab.phrase: to know when to step away
hintVocab.meaningVi: biết khi nào nên rút lui
sự rút lui được sắp xếp

hintVocab.phrase: to be patronised by an audience
hintVocab.meaningVi: bị khán giả coi như đáng thương

sampleText:
Watching a celebrated Cairo singer at her final scheduled concert last spring, one began to understand that the people in the audience were not there to hear the voice she had in 1978. They were there to hear the voice she has now — thinner, slower, more carefully placed, sometimes audibly imperfect — and to be in a room with a woman who has spent fifty-one years giving them their language back. Whether such a performer ought to have stepped away earlier is a question that asks itself, but not as easily as the criticism makes it sound.

What an ageing performer's last decade on stage offers an audience that no younger performer can replicate is the slow, gathered weight of an interpretation that has been made by the same body and the same memory for half a century. A young soprano can outsing the old one on any technical measure. The young soprano cannot, by definition, carry the years of grief, marriage, exile, and quiet practice into the second verse of an aria the older singer first recorded in 1976. The lost top notes are real. They are also not the only thing the audience came for.

Granted, the line between dignified late-career performance and the slow public unravelling of a once-great voice is not always easy to draw. There are tenors who should have stopped a decade before they did. There are athletes whose final seasons looked, in retrospect, like a long, sad public injury. To pretend that no performer ever stays on too long is to ignore what loyal audiences have actually had to sit through. The risk of overstaying one's welcome is not imaginary.

Even so, the right to make that decision belongs primarily to the performer, not to the audience or the management. A career is not a public utility. An artist who has spent a lifetime giving their work to others is entitled to decide, on their own terms, when that work ends — including the right to keep going for one more season, in front of an audience that knows exactly what it is buying.

Admittedly, that right is not unlimited. Where a performance becomes physically dangerous to the performer, or where management is exploiting a fading star against their own interests, others have a duty to intervene. A dancer who is being pushed onto a stage because the box office still depends on her name is being treated as a brand, not as a person. The opposite of forced retirement is not the absence of care.

What an ageing performer's continued presence on stage finally gives an audience is not a perfect performance. It is something rarer: the chance to watch a working life held all the way to its end by the person who built it, and to be present for the kind of farewell that takes more than one evening to complete.

sampleAnnotations.phrase: Whether such a performer ought to have stepped away earlier is a question that asks itself, but not as easily as the criticism makes it sound

sampleAnnotations.phrase: What an ageing performer's last decade on stage offers an audience that no younger performer can replicate is the slow, gathered weight of an interpretation that has been made by the same body and the same memory for half a century

sampleAnnotations.phrase: The lost top notes are real. They are also not the only thing the audience came for

sampleAnnotations.phrase: There are tenors who should have stopped a decade before they did

sampleAnnotations.phrase: What an ageing performer's continued presence on stage finally gives an audience is not a perfect performance

sampleAnnotations.phrase: to be present for the kind of farewell that takes more than one evening to complete

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'What' cleft to name an irreplaceable artistic value
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What an ageing performer's last decade on stage offers an audience that no younger performer can replicate is the slow, gathered weight of an interpretation that has been made by the same body and the same memory for half a century' dùng cấu trúc 'What … is …' kết hợp với mệnh đề quan hệ chèn vào ('that no younger performer can replicate') để tách bạch giá trị mà chỉ có nghệ sĩ già mới có thể mang lại. Phần sau 'is' chứa nội dung trừu tượng phức tạp được nén vào một cụm danh từ.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What an ageing performer's last decade on stage offers an audience that no younger performer can replicate is the slow, gathered weight of an interpretation that has been made by the same body and the same memory for half a century' uses a what-cleft with an embedded relative clause ('that no younger performer can replicate') to isolate the value only an old performer can give. The clause after 'is' carries dense abstract content compressed into a single noun phrase.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Continuous passive within a defining relative for moral diagnosis
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'A dancer who is being pushed onto a stage because the box office still depends on her name is being treated as a brand, not as a person' chứa hai dạng bị động tiếp diễn ('is being pushed', 'is being treated'). Cấu trúc 'who is being pushed…' chèn vào trong chủ ngữ, và cấu trúc song song 'as a brand, not as a person' ở vế chính nén toàn bộ luận điểm đạo đức vào một câu duy nhất.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'A dancer who is being pushed onto a stage because the box office still depends on her name is being treated as a brand, not as a person' contains two continuous passives ('is being pushed', 'is being treated'). The 'who is being pushed…' clause sits inside the subject, and the parallel 'as a brand, not as a person' in the main clause compresses the moral diagnosis into one sentence.

sampleTranslationVi:
Xem một ca sĩ nổi tiếng ở Cairo tại buổi hòa nhạc cuối được lên lịch của bà vào mùa xuân năm ngoái, người ta bắt đầu hiểu rằng những người trong khán phòng không ở đó để nghe giọng hát mà bà đã có vào năm 1978. Họ ở đó để nghe giọng hát mà bà có bây giờ — mỏng hơn, chậm hơn, được đặt cẩn thận hơn, đôi khi không hoàn hảo có thể nghe thấy — và để được ở trong một căn phòng với một người phụ nữ đã dành năm mươi mốt năm trao lại cho họ ngôn ngữ của họ. Liệu một nghệ sĩ như vậy có nên rút lui sớm hơn hay không là một câu hỏi tự nó đặt ra, nhưng không dễ dàng như lời phê bình khiến nó nghe có vẻ.

Điều mà thập kỷ cuối cùng trên sân khấu của một nghệ sĩ lớn tuổi mang đến cho khán giả mà không một nghệ sĩ trẻ nào có thể tái tạo là sức nặng chậm rãi, tích lũy của một cách diễn giải đã được thực hiện bởi cùng một cơ thể và cùng một ký ức trong nửa thế kỷ. Một giọng nữ cao trẻ có thể hát vượt qua người cũ theo bất kỳ thước đo kỹ thuật nào. Giọng nữ cao trẻ, theo định nghĩa, không thể mang theo những năm tháng đau buồn, hôn nhân, lưu vong, và luyện tập lặng lẽ vào đoạn hai của một bản aria mà ca sĩ lớn tuổi đã thu âm lần đầu vào năm 1976. Những nốt cao bị mất là có thật. Chúng cũng không phải là điều duy nhất khán giả đến để nghe.

Thừa nhận rằng, ranh giới giữa biểu diễn cuối sự nghiệp đáng kính và sự bóc lộ công khai chậm rãi của một giọng hát từng vĩ đại không phải lúc nào cũng dễ vạch ra. Có những giọng nam cao đáng lẽ phải dừng trước đó một thập kỷ. Có những vận động viên có những mùa cuối nhìn lại trông giống như một chấn thương công cộng dài và đáng buồn. Giả vờ rằng không một nghệ sĩ nào ở lại quá lâu là bỏ qua những gì các khán giả trung thành đã thực sự phải chịu đựng. Nguy cơ ở lại quá thời gian được hoan nghênh không phải là tưởng tượng.

Dẫu vậy, quyền đưa ra quyết định đó chủ yếu thuộc về nghệ sĩ, không phải khán giả hay ban quản lý. Một sự nghiệp không phải là một tiện ích công cộng. Một nghệ sĩ đã dành cả cuộc đời cống hiến tác phẩm của mình cho người khác có quyền quyết định, theo điều kiện riêng của họ, khi nào tác phẩm đó kết thúc — bao gồm cả quyền tiếp tục thêm một mùa nữa, trước một khán giả biết chính xác họ đang mua gì.

Thừa nhận rằng, quyền đó không phải là không giới hạn. Khi một buổi biểu diễn trở nên nguy hiểm về mặt thể chất đối với nghệ sĩ, hay khi ban quản lý đang bóc lột một ngôi sao đang phai nhạt chống lại chính lợi ích của họ, những người khác có nghĩa vụ can thiệp. Một vũ công đang bị đẩy lên sân khấu vì doanh thu phòng vé vẫn phụ thuộc vào tên của bà đang bị đối xử như một thương hiệu, không phải một con người. Đối lập của sự nghỉ hưu cưỡng bức không phải là sự thiếu vắng quan tâm.

Điều mà sự hiện diện tiếp tục trên sân khấu của một nghệ sĩ lớn tuổi cuối cùng mang đến cho khán giả không phải là một buổi biểu diễn hoàn hảo. Đó là điều hiếm hơn: cơ hội được xem một cuộc sống làm việc được giữ vững đến tận cuối bởi chính người đã xây dựng nó, và có mặt cho loại lời chia tay cần hơn một buổi tối để hoàn thành.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a 'what' cleft to isolate what only an older performer can offer?
options: The slow gathered weight of interpretation by the same body and memory for half a century what an ageing performer's last decade offers.

What an ageing performer's last decade on stage offers an audience that no younger performer can replicate is the slow, gathered weight of an interpretation that has been made by the same body and the same memory for half a century.
An ageing performer's last decade what offers on stage is half a century the slow weight of interpretation made by the same body memory.
Has been made by the same body memory half a century the slow weight what an ageing performer's last decade offers is.
mcQuestions.explanation: The cleft 'What … is …' delays the long descriptive noun phrase to the emphatic position; the embedded relative ('that no younger performer can replicate') sharpens the irreplaceable claim.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'What … is …' with a long, complex noun phrase placed after 'is'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to overstay one's welcome on stage' mean?
options: To physically remain on stage after a performance has ended
To continue performing for longer than is artistically or personally wise, after the audience has begun to wish one had stopped
To stay later at a party after the host has gone to bed
To exceed the booked time of a venue's rental contract
mcQuestions.explanation: The phrase captures the discomfort of a career extended past the point at which an audience would have preferred a dignified end.
mcQuestions.hint: What does 'overstay one's welcome' mean in general, and how would that apply to a performer's career?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'granted' correctly as a concession?
options: Granted, the line between dignified late-career performance and the slow public unravelling of a once-great voice is not always easy to draw.
The line granted between dignified late-career performance not always easy to draw is and the slow public unravelling.
Not always easy to draw granted the line between dignified late-career performance and the slow public unravelling is.
Performance dignified late-career granted between the line and the slow public unravelling not always easy to draw is.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted,' followed by a complete clause cleanly grants a real point to the opposing view.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option places 'Granted,' before a full, grammatical sentence?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses two continuous passives to make a moral diagnosis?
options: A dancer who is being pushed onto a stage because the box office still depends on her name is being treated as a brand, not as a person.
Being pushed onto a stage a dancer the box office still depends on her name being treated as a brand not as a person.
Onto a stage being pushed a dancer being treated her name still depends as a brand not as a person the box office.
Being treated as a brand a dancer is who being pushed onto a stage not as a person the box office depends on her name still.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'is being pushed' and 'is being treated' are both continuous passives; combined, they expose an ongoing manipulation and an ongoing dehumanisation in one sentence.
mcQuestions.hint: Continuous passive looks like 'is/are being + past participle'. Look for two of these in the same sentence.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'diminished powers' mean for a performer?
options: A reduction in their salary by the management
A measurable decline in their physical or technical abilities — voice, breath, mobility — that the audience can perceive
A loss of their legal rights as a performer
A reduction in the wattage of their stage lighting
mcQuestions.explanation: Diminished powers refers to the visible falling away of the physical capacities that once defined the performer at their peak — the high notes, the speed, the stamina.
mcQuestions.hint: What kind of 'powers' does a performer have, and what does 'diminished' do to them?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option best paraphrases 'A career is not a public utility'?
options: A performer's career is regulated like the electricity supply.
A performer's career does not exist to serve public convenience on demand, and is not owed to the audience.
Public utilities should be run by performers.
A career is a service that anyone can access for a fee.
mcQuestions.explanation: The metaphor is rejected by the writer: a career belongs to the person who built it, not to the audience that consumes it; the performer is not obliged to retire on the public's timeline.
mcQuestions.hint: What is the essay arguing about who the performer's career belongs to?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to be patronised by an audience' mean for an ageing performer?
options: To receive paid patronage from wealthy audience members
To be treated by the audience as someone deserving of pity rather than respect — politely tolerated rather than genuinely engaged
To attract patrons who are willing to fund future performances
To be praised generously by a loyal audience
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Patronise' in this sense means to treat someone as inferior under a veneer of kindness — a real risk for an ageing performer whose audience has begun to feel sorry for them.
mcQuestions.hint: What does 'patronise' mean when it carries a negative meaning rather than a financial one?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a participle clause as an opener to describe the scene?
options: Watching a celebrated Cairo singer at her final scheduled concert last spring, one began to understand that the people in the audience were not there to hear the voice she had in 1978.
A celebrated Cairo singer one began to understand watching at her final scheduled concert last spring the people in the audience were not there to hear.
The voice she had in 1978 watching a celebrated Cairo singer at her final scheduled concert last spring the people were not there to hear.
In the audience the people last spring watching a celebrated Cairo singer at her final scheduled concert one began to understand were not there to hear the voice she had in 1978.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Watching a celebrated Cairo singer at her final scheduled concert last spring' is a present-participle clause acting as an opener — it sets the observational frame before the main clause delivers the insight.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for an -ing phrase at the start of the sentence, separated by a comma from the main clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best signals a return to the main argument after a concession?
options: For example,
Even so, the right to make that decision belongs primarily to the performer, not to the audience or the management.
On the contrary, no performer has ever stayed too long.
Furthermore, all performers must continue forever.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so' grants the previous concession (some performers really do stay too long) and pivots to the writer's main argument about who has the right to decide.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrase both accepts the concession and reasserts the writer's position?

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's central position?
options: All ageing performers should be forced to retire by age sixty.
Ageing performers should be allowed to perform forever, with no oversight at all.

The decision about when to retire belongs primarily to the performer, but others have a duty to intervene when continuing performance becomes physically dangerous or when management is exploiting a fading star.
Audiences should pretend not to notice when a performer is in decline.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay defends the performer's right to decide, while explicitly carving out two narrow exceptions: physical danger and exploitation by management.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at paragraphs four and five for the writer's defence and its specific limits.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence maintains the most formal academic register?
options: Honestly some old singers really should just quit, they're embarrassing themselves.
Older performers are basically heroes and we should just let them sing forever.
The decision to conclude a public-performance career belongs in the first instance to the performer, subject only to constraints rooted in physical safety or exploitative management.
It's kind of mean to push people off the stage just because they got older, full stop.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C uses formal vocabulary ('public-performance career', 'in the first instance', 'rooted in physical safety or exploitative management') and avoids contractions, slang, and informal intensifiers.

options: The first performance of a young singer's career
A final, often poignant, performance or work by an artist before retirement or death
A traditional folk song about waterbirds
A song performed in honour of a recently deceased composer
mcQuestions.explanation: Swansong comes from the ancient belief that swans sing beautifully just before dying; it now means an artist's final, often emotionally weighted, performance.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'swan' + 'song' as a literary tradition about endings.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a defining relative clause?
options: An artist who has spent a lifetime giving their work to others is entitled to decide, on their own terms, when that work ends.
Who has spent a lifetime giving their work to others an artist is entitled to decide on their own terms when that work ends.
Is entitled to decide an artist on their own terms when that work ends who has spent a lifetime giving their work to others.
To decide on their own terms when that work ends an artist who has spent a lifetime giving their work to others is entitled.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'who has spent a lifetime giving their work to others' is a defining relative clause specifying exactly which artist the essay means.
mcQuestions.hint: Defining relative clauses follow the noun directly and start with 'who', 'that', or 'which'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'a working life held all the way to its end by the person who built it' mean?
options: A career that lasted longer than expected by the medical profession
A career that the artist themselves continues to direct and control until the end, rather than handing it over to managers or to audience expectations
A career whose retirement was financially planned in advance
A career managed by an external life coach
mcQuestions.explanation: The phrase captures the writer's central image: the dignity of an artist who remains the author of their own ending, rather than being timed out by external forces.
mcQuestions.hint: Who is doing the holding, and what is being held?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best closes the essay by redefining what an ageing performer offers?
options: In conclusion, performers should retire when they want to.
Therefore, all retirement decisions must be made by management.

What an ageing performer's continued presence on stage finally gives an audience is not a perfect performance. It is something rarer: the chance to watch a working life held all the way to its end by the person who built it, and to be present for the kind of farewell that takes more than one evening to complete.
To sum up, performers age and that is sad.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C reframes the question — not perfection, but witness to a self-authored ending — in a memorable two-sentence movement.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option states what the performer's continued presence actually offers, rather than vaguely summarising?

mcQuestions.prompt: What two narrow exceptions does the essay name to the performer's right to keep going?
options: Audience boredom and falling ticket sales
Physical danger to the performer, and management exploiting a fading star against their own interests
Critical reviews and union complaints
Tax issues and copyright disputes
mcQuestions.explanation: Paragraph five names exactly these two narrow conditions under which others have a duty to intervene.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at paragraph five for the writer's specific exceptions.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of an ageing performer, athlete, or public figure whose continued presence in their field has been criticised. What would you actually need to know about their motivations, their physical condition, and their management arrangement before you felt entitled to an opinion about whether they should retire?
